preamble: The transformation from a voiceless aspirated alveolar plosive (tʰ) to a voiced alveolar flap (ɾ) is a prominent allophonic variation of the phoneme /t/ in several dialects of English, including North American and Australian English. This phenomenon, commonly known as T-flapping, involves the lenition of the alveolar stop when it occurs in specific phonetic environments, typically between a stressed vowel and an unstressed vowel or syllabic consonant.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (weakening of a stop to a flap), deaspiration (loss of aspiration), and voicing (change from voiceless to voiced)

examples.note: The underlying /t/ phoneme is realized as an aspirated plosive [tʰ] in the stressed syllable of 'photographer' but as a voiced alveolar flap [ɾ] in the unstressed intervocalic position of 'photograph'.
The /t/ phoneme is aspirated [tʰ] when stressed in 'atomic' but flapped [ɾ] when unstressed and intervocalic in 'atom'.

sources: Linguistics Network. (2015). An Introduction to Phonology.
Schaefer, R. P., & Darcy, S. (2014). Second Language Acquisition: An Advanced Resource Book. Routledge.
